sections.Healthcare-associated infections in the: Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) are serious complications in the ICU, where patients are uniquely vulnerable due to age, comorbidities, and immunosuppression. Device-associated infections — VAP, CRBSI, and CAUTI — are the dominant infectious complications. Colonisation is the presence of multiplying pathogens without tissue damage or clinical symptoms; not every colonisation leads to infection, and systemic antibiotics do not prevent colonisation (they select for resistance). Up to 65–70% of CRBSI and CAUTI, and ~55% of VAP cases, are preventable. Prevention requires hand hygiene, environmental disinfection, care bundles, staff education, and HAI surveillance. An unfavourable nurse-to-patient ratio independently increases HAI risk.
sections.Ventilator-associated pneumonia: VAP affects up to 50% of mechanically ventilated patients; acquisition risk is 3% per day in week 1, 2% in week 2, and 1% per day thereafter. Attributable mortality is 3–10% (relative mortality risk increase ~25%); false-positive diagnoses occur in 20–30% of cases. Essential VAP prevention: avoid intubation/reintubation, minimise sedation, semi-recumbent positioning (30–45°), oral care with tooth brushing WITHOUT chlorhexidine (CHX oral care reclassified 'not recommended' — meta-analyses suggest increased mortality), early enteral nutrition within 48–72h, change ventilator circuits only when visibly soiled. Additional approaches: SDD/SOD (only in ICUs with <5% ESBL BSI), subglottic secretion aspiration (for expected MV >48–72h), early tracheostomy, post-pyloric feeding. NOT recommended: CHX oral care, probiotics, silver-coated ETTs, closed suctioning systems, prone positioning for VAP, stress-ulcer prophylaxis, early parenteral nutrition. VAE framework: VAC (daily minimum PEEP increase ≥3 cmH2O sustained ≥2 days after ≥2 days stable, OR FiO2 increase ≥20 percentage points sustained ≥2 days); IVAC (VAC + fever/WBC abnormality + new antibiotics ≥4 days); Possible VAP (IVAC + positive respiratory cultures/assays).
sections.Catheter-related bloodstream infections: CRBSIs carry high mortality. Most common route: skin inoculation along the subcutaneous tract during insertion; secondary route: hub contamination. Prevention: avoid femoral site (higher CRBSI than subclavian), use maximal barrier precautions, chlorhexidine 2% (superior to povidone-iodine), do NOT perform guidewire exchange for CRBSI or site infections, daily review of CVC necessity, dedicate one CVC lumen to PN. Antimicrobial catheters: minocycline-rifampicin more durable (>3 weeks) than silver sulfadiazine-CHX; primary concern is AMR selection, not cost. Blood cultures: simultaneous peripheral + CVC samples per IDSA, 8–10 mL/bottle, incubation <12h; Staph, Gram-negatives, and Candida in any single BC set should be treated as true infection. Prophylactic antibiotics are NOT recommended.
sections.Catheter-associated urinary tract infect: CAUTI: lower direct mortality than VAP/CRBSI. Most important modifiable risk factor: catheter dwell time (bacteriuria approaches 100% after 30+ days). Non-modifiable: female sex (shorter urethra), age, diabetes, structural urologic abnormalities. Diagnosis: CAUTI is a clinical diagnosis; bacteriuria and pyuria alone are insufficient in catheterised patients. Urine appearance/odour are NOT diagnostic criteria. Routine surveillance cultures are NOT recommended. Specimen collection: remove catheter if possible (midstream); replace catheter >10–14 days old before sampling; sample from aspiration port if catheter must remain and is recently placed. NEVER collect from the drainage bag. Prevention: daily reassessment, aseptic insertion, closed drainage system, bag always below bladder level, no routine catheter replacement at fixed intervals. Nurse-driven protocols (NDPs) are superior to physician-directed management for reducing catheter use and CAUTI.

questions.question: A 74-year-old mechanically ventilated patient has a BAL culture growing 10³ CFU/mL of Pseudomonas aeruginosa on day 5 of ICU admission. He is afebrile, has no new radiological infiltrate, WBC is 8,400/mm³, and there has been no change in ventilator requirements. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: A: No antibiotic therapy; this reflects colonisation, not infection
B: Start anti-pseudomonal antibiotics immediately to prevent progression to VAP
C: Start inhaled colistin to decolonise the airway without systemic antibiotic selection pressure
D: Request serum procalcitonin trended over 48 hours to help guide the antibiotic treatment decision
E: Repeat BAL culture in 48 hours before initiating antibiotics
questions.explanation: Colonisation is the presence of multiplying pathogens without tissue damage, immune response, or clinical symptoms. This patient has no clinical or radiological criteria for VAP. Treating colonisation does not prevent progression to infection and actively promotes AMR. Starting antibiotics for this low colony count without clinical signs is the most dangerous near-miss — it does not meet VAP criteria. Inhaled colistin to 'decolonise' the airway has no evidence base for colonisation prevention and would similarly worsen resistance selection.
questions.topic: colonisation infection distinction VAP antibiotics AMR selection

questions.question: An ICU governance committee is reviewing HAI prevention targets. According to current evidence, what proportion of catheter-related bloodstream infections (CRBSI) and catheter-associated urinary tract infections (CAUTI) are potentially preventable through consistent application of evidence-based strategies?
options: A: More than 80%
B: Up to 65–70%
D: Up to 55%
questions.explanation: Up to 65–70% of CRBSI and CAUTI cases are preventable with consistent evidence-based bundle application. The 'up to 55%' figure is the near-miss: this correctly applies to VAP, not CRBSI/CAUTI — highlighting the importance of infection-specific prevention benchmarks. Neither the lower estimates nor the more-than-80% figure is supported by the evidence.
questions.topic: HAI preventable proportion CRBSI CAUTI 65-70% VAP 55% benchmark

questions.question: Following a sustained rise in CAUTI rates over six months in a surgical ICU, infection control audits reveal no change in insertion technique or maintenance protocols. Which organisational factor is most independently associated with increased HAI risk?
options: A: Increased average patient age and comorbidity burden over the review period
B: Absence of a dedicated infection control nurse
C: Inadequate electronic HAI surveillance infrastructure
D: Reduced frequency of medical education sessions on HAI prevention
E: Unfavourable nurse-to-patient ratio
questions.explanation: An unfavourable nurse-to-patient ratio is independently associated with a significantly increased risk of healthcare-associated infections, directly impacting hand hygiene compliance, catheter assessments, and care bundle adherence. Rising patient age and comorbidity burden is a plausible confounding variable but is not the organisational factor with established independent association in the evidence. The remaining systems-level explanations lack the same level of independent evidence.
questions.topic: nurse-to-patient ratio HAI independent risk organisational staffing

questions.question: A 68-year-old with ARDS has been mechanically ventilated for 10 days. A trainee asks about the daily VAP acquisition rate over the course of mechanical ventilation. Which of the following best describes this risk?
options: A: 5% per day for the first 5 days, then declining sharply to less than 1% per day after day 10 of ventilation
B: Constant at approximately 1% per day throughout mechanical ventilation
C: Constant at approximately 3% per day throughout the first two weeks, then declining
D: 2% per day for the first week, increasing after day 14
E: 3% per day during week 1, decreasing to 2% per day in week 2, and 1% per day thereafter
questions.explanation: VAP acquisition risk follows a step-down pattern: 3% per day in week 1, 2% per day in week 2, and 1% per day thereafter — reflecting early susceptibility in the most vulnerable patients. Describing a constant 3% rate through the first two weeks is the most common near-miss: it correctly identifies the initial rate but fails to account for the progressive decline. A constant 1% risk throughout underestimates early risk and would lead to under-prioritisation of prevention in the critical first week.
questions.topic: VAP daily acquisition rate epidemiology 3% 2% 1% per day week

questions.question: A critical care nurse proposes adding chlorhexidine oral care to the ICU's VAP prevention bundle for all mechanically ventilated patients. Based on current guidelines, what is the most accurate statement?
options: A: Chlorhexidine oral care is an essential, guideline-endorsed practice that consistently reduces VAP rates across all ventilated patients and should be adopted hospital-wide
B: Chlorhexidine oral care is recommended only in combination with selective oral decontamination protocols
C: Chlorhexidine oral care has been reclassified as 'not recommended' because meta-analyses suggest it may increase mortality
D: Chlorhexidine oral care and tooth brushing are equivalent alternatives and either can be selected based on availability
E: Chlorhexidine oral care reduces VAP rates but should be reserved for high-risk patients on prolonged ventilation
questions.explanation: Oral care with chlorhexidine has been reclassified as 'not recommended' in current guidelines because meta-analyses of randomised trials suggest it may increase mortality. Daily tooth brushing without chlorhexidine is the evidence-based essential practice, associated with reduced VAP rates and shorter ICU stay. Recommending its adoption as an essential practice is the most dangerous near-miss, reflecting widely implemented older protocols that are now outdated. Reserving it for high-risk patients, treating it as equivalent to plain tooth brushing, or pairing it with selective decontamination all represent common clinical misconceptions about selective or combined use.
questions.topic: chlorhexidine oral care not recommended mortality increase tooth brushing essential VAP

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT classified as an essential VAP prevention practice in current guidelines?
options: A: Minimising sedation through targeted protocols coupled with daily spontaneous breathing trials to assess extubation readiness
B: Subglottic secretion aspiration for patients expected to require mechanical ventilation for more than 48–72 hours
C: Early enteral nutrition within 48–72 hours of onset of critical illness
D: Avoiding intubation and promoting non-invasive respiratory support strategies whenever clinically feasible
E: Semi-recumbent positioning at 30–45° to the horizontal
questions.explanation: Subglottic secretion aspiration is classified as an additional approach, not an essential practice. It may reduce VAP rates but insufficient data exist to demonstrate impact on duration of MV, ICU length of stay, or mortality. Avoiding intubation, minimising sedation, semi-recumbent positioning, and early enteral nutrition are all essential practices. Early enteral nutrition is the closest near-miss: some clinicians classify nutrition as supportive rather than infection-preventive, but early EN is itself an essential VAP prevention strategy.
questions.topic: VAP prevention NOT essential subglottic aspiration additional approach classification

questions.question: A mechanically ventilated patient has been stable on PEEP 5 cmH2O and FiO2 0.40 for 3 days. On days 4 and 5, the minimum daily PEEP increases to 9 cmH2O. The patient is afebrile, WBC is normal, and no new antibiotics have been started. Which CDC Ventilator-Associated Event (VAE) tier does this patient meet?
options: A: Ventilator-Associated Condition (VAC) — the PEEP increase meets the required threshold and duration criteria
B: VAE surveillance is not applicable until mechanical ventilation has exceeded 14 days
C: No VAE criteria are met — the PEEP increase must exceed 5 cmH2O from baseline for VAC
D: Infection-related Ventilator-Associated Complication (IVAC) — new antibiotics are required to confirm this tier
E: Possible VAP — a new radiological infiltrate is the only missing criterion
questions.explanation: VAC requires a sustained daily minimum PEEP increase of ≥3 cmH2O for ≥2 calendar days following ≥2 days of stable PEEP. Here PEEP rises by 4 cmH2O (5→9), sustained for 2 days after 3 days of stability — meeting VAC criteria. IVAC additionally requires signs of infection (abnormal temperature or WBC) and new antibiotics ≥4 days, which are absent here. Claiming the PEEP rise must exceed 5 cmH2O is the critical near-miss: the actual threshold is ≥3 cmH2O — a rise of 4 cmH2O clearly fulfils the criterion.
questions.topic: VAC VAE CDC criteria PEEP ≥3 cmH2O threshold duration IVAC not met

questions.question: A quality improvement committee proposes implementing selective digestive decontamination (SDD) to reduce VAP rates. According to current guidelines, which of the following best describes the appropriate indication for SDD?
options: A: Any ICU that has maintained a standard VAP bundle for more than 12 months without achieving a ≥50% rate reduction
B: ICUs with more than 30% of patients mechanically ventilated for more than 7 days
C: Any ICU with a VAP rate exceeding 5 per 1,000 ventilator-days regardless of local AMR epidemiology
D: ICUs in countries where fewer than 5% of bloodstream infections are caused by ESBL-producing pathogens
E: ICUs in countries where more than 5% of bloodstream infections are caused by ESBL-producing pathogens
questions.explanation: SDD/SOD are recommended only in ICUs in countries where fewer than 5% of bloodstream infections are caused by ESBL-producing pathogens. A clustered RCT in high-AMR settings showed no benefit. Inverting the threshold to 'more than 5% ESBL' is the critical near-miss: high-AMR environments represent a contraindication, not an indication. The remaining options use non-evidence-based indications unrelated to the AMR-based guideline criterion.
questions.topic: SDD SOD AMR threshold ESBL 5% low resistance guideline indication

questions.question: During CVC insertion training, a registrar asks which skin antiseptic to use. Which of the following is the most evidence-based recommendation?
options: A: Chlorhexidine 0.5% in alcohol, which has equivalent efficacy to the 2% formulation
B: Alternating povidone-iodine and chlorhexidine on successive insertions to maximise broad-spectrum antisepsis
C: Chlorhexidine 2% in alcohol, allowed to dry
D: Alcohol 70% alone, as it achieves faster complete skin sterilisation than chlorhexidine
E: Povidone-iodine 10% applied twice and allowed to air-dry, as it has the broadest bactericidal spectrum
questions.explanation: Chlorhexidine 2% is more effective than povidone-iodine as a skin antiseptic for CVC insertion and is the evidence-based recommendation. Recommending povidone-iodine is the near-miss: it was previously standard practice but evidence demonstrates CHX 2% reduces CRBSI rates more effectively. The 2% concentration is specifically recommended and is not equivalent to a 0.5% formulation.
questions.topic: CVC insertion skin antiseptic chlorhexidine 2% superior povidone-iodine CRBSI

questions.question: A 55-year-old in septic shock requires emergency CVC insertion. Which insertion site carries the highest risk of CRBSI and should be avoided as the primary choice?
options: A: Femoral vein
B: Right subclavian vein
C: Left subclavian vein
D: Basilic vein (PICC line)
E: Left internal jugular vein
questions.explanation: The femoral site is associated with the highest incidence of CRBSI among standard central venous access sites, and avoiding femoral cannulation is specifically recommended. The internal jugular vein is a common near-miss: it carries intermediate risk and is an acceptable first-line site, but the question asks for the site with the highest CRBSI risk. Subclavian access carries the lowest CRBSI rates and is preferred when technically feasible.
questions.topic: CRBSI insertion site femoral highest risk subclavian lowest internal jugular

questions.question: A patient's CVC insertion site develops erythema and tenderness on day 8 with no fever and negative blood cultures. A trainee suggests a guidewire exchange to preserve vascular access and avoid a new puncture. What is the most appropriate response?
options: A: Guidewire exchange is appropriate only after excluding MRSA colonisation at the insertion site
B: Guidewire exchange is not recommended; the catheter should be removed and a new site selected
C: Guidewire exchange is the recommended approach to avoid opening a new insertion site
D: Guidewire exchange is acceptable in patients with limited vascular access to reduce complications
E: Guidewire exchange is appropriate if repeat blood cultures at 24 hours remain negative
questions.explanation: Current guidelines explicitly state that guidewire exchange should not be performed for CRBSI or catheter-related insertion site infections; the catheter should be removed and a new site used. Justifying guidewire exchange on the basis of limited vascular access is the most clinically tempting near-miss: it is frequently invoked as a pragmatic argument, but this reasoning is not guideline-supported and risks seeding the new catheter with organisms colonising the infected subcutaneous tract.
questions.topic: guidewire exchange not recommended CRBSI catheter removal new site insertion

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended strategy for reducing catheter-related bloodstream infections (CRBSI) in the ICU?
options: A: Daily review and documentation of the indication for each in-situ CVC
B: Using chlorhexidine 2% rather than povidone-iodine for skin antisepsis
C: Avoiding the femoral site as the primary CVC insertion location
D: Routine replacement of CVCs at fixed intervals (every 7 days)
E: Maximal sterile barrier precautions during insertion, including sterile gown, large drapes, and sterile gloves
questions.explanation: Routine replacement of CVCs at fixed time intervals is NOT recommended to prevent CRBSI. While colonisation probability increases with time (notably after 5–7 days), the correct approach is daily reassessment and removal at the earliest clinical opportunity — not scheduled exchanges. Avoiding the femoral site, chlorhexidine 2% skin antisepsis, maximal sterile barrier precautions, and daily review of CVC indication are all evidence-based CRBSI prevention measures; daily review in particular may appear optional but is specifically recommended and reduces CVC dwell time.
questions.topic: CRBSI NOT recommended routine CVC replacement fixed interval guideline

questions.question: A mechanically ventilated 72-year-old has had an indwelling urinary catheter for 14 days. A routine urine culture shows 10⁵ CFU/mL of E. coli with pyuria (50 WBC/hpf). The patient is afebrile, haemodynamically stable, and has no localising urinary or systemic signs of infection. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: A: Start nitrofurantoin for 7 days as first-line CAUTI treatment in catheterised patients
B: Request urinary PCR testing to confirm viable bacterial load before initiating any antibiotic therapy
C: Replace the urinary catheter and repeat the urine culture; treat only if the repeat is positive
D: Start ciprofloxacin immediately for CAUTI based on significant bacteriuria and pyuria
E: No antibiotic therapy; bacteriuria and pyuria do not confirm CAUTI without systemic signs
questions.explanation: CAUTI is a clinical diagnosis requiring integration of bacteriuria, pyuria, and localising or systemic signs of infection. In catheterised patients, bacteriuria and pyuria are common features of colonisation and are insufficient alone. Routine surveillance cultures are explicitly not recommended for this reason. Starting antibiotics immediately is the most dangerous near-miss: treating asymptomatic bacteriuria in a catheterised ICU patient promotes AMR without clinical benefit. Replacing the catheter and repeating the culture seems pragmatic but is not guideline-supported — catheter replacement without clinical CAUTI criteria is unnecessary.
questions.topic: CAUTI diagnosis bacteriuria pyuria insufficient clinical diagnosis asymptomatic no antibiotics

questions.question: Which of the following is the most important modifiable risk factor for catheter-associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI)?
options: A: Diabetes mellitus
B: Pre-existing structural urological abnormalities
C: Catheter dwell time
D: Female sex due to the anatomically shorter urethra
E: Patient age greater than 65 years
questions.explanation: Catheter dwell time is the most important modifiable risk factor for CAUTI; bacteriuria approaches a 100% likelihood after 30 or more days of catheterisation. Female sex, age, and structural urological abnormalities are non-modifiable host-level risk factors. Diabetes increases susceptibility but is not the dominant modifiable driver in catheterised patients. Daily assessment and early catheter removal are therefore the cornerstone of CAUTI prevention strategy.
questions.topic: CAUTI modifiable risk factor catheter dwell time bacteriuria 100% 30 days

questions.question: An ICU governance review identifies prolonged catheter dwell time as the primary driver of its CAUTI rate. Which quality improvement strategy has the strongest evidence for reducing both catheter use and CAUTI incidence?
options: A: Mandatory daily physician-led catheter rounds with structured documentation
B: Automated electronic reminders to the treating physician regarding catheter necessity
C: Mandatory annual training sessions for all clinical staff on CAUTI diagnostic criteria
D: Nurse-driven protocols with pre-defined criteria allowing removal without a physician order
E: Weekly multidisciplinary review of urine culture results and catheter indications from all catheterised patients
questions.explanation: Nurse-driven protocols (NDPs) are specifically evidenced to reduce both catheter use and CAUTI incidence compared to physician-directed catheter management. NDPs formalise the nurse's authority to assess necessity and initiate removal based on pre-defined criteria, removing the bottleneck of awaiting physician orders. Structured physician-led rounds are the near-miss: beneficial, but structurally less effective than NDPs. Automated reminders to the treating physician rely on physician responsiveness and lack the autonomous structure of NDPs.
questions.topic: nurse-driven protocols CAUTI reduction catheter removal physician order autonomy

questions.question: Regarding healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) and the distinction between colonisation and infection in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Bacteriuria occurring during urinary catheterisation is a common example of colonisation rather than true infection.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Systemic antimicrobial therapy is effective in preventing colonisation from progressing to true infection.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Healthcare-associated infections are defined by the presence of infection at or before the time of hospital admission.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Up to 55% of VAP cases and up to 65–70% of CRBSI and CAUTI cases are potentially preventable through consistent application of evidence-based strategies.

questions.explanation: HAIs are, by definition, NOT present or incubating at the time of admission — the claim that they are is false. Systemic antibiotics do not prevent colonisation from progressing to infection; they select for resistance instead, so that claim is also false. The preventability benchmarks (55% VAP, 65–70% CRBSI/CAUTI) and bacteriuria as a colonisation example are both correct.
questions.topic: HAI definition colonisation infection preventable proportion antibiotics AMR selection

questions.question: Regarding the epidemiology and diagnosis of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP):

statements.label: A
statements.text: Current guidelines recommend re-evaluating the pneumonia diagnosis after 72 hours of treatment initiation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: False-positive VAP diagnoses may occur in up to 20–30% of cases, which can inflate apparent mortality data.

statements.label: C
statements.text: VAP affects up to 50% of patients receiving invasive mechanical ventilation.

statements.label: D
statements.text: The attributable mortality of VAP is approximately 25–30%.

questions.explanation: Attributable mortality from VAP is 3–10%, not 25–30% — the 25% figure refers to the relative increase in mortality risk, a clinically important distinction and the most common near-miss here. The up-to-50% incidence figure, the 20–30% false-positive rate inflating mortality data, and the 72-hour re-evaluation recommendation are all correct.
questions.topic: VAP incidence attributable mortality 3-10% false positive 72h re-evaluation

questions.question: Regarding the CDC Ventilator-Associated Events (VAE) surveillance framework:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A sustained FiO2 increase of 15 percentage points for 2 calendar days after 2 days of stability meets criteria for a VAC.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The VAE framework was developed to overcome the diagnostic subjectivity inherent in traditional VAP surveillance definitions.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A positive BAL culture is an essential criterion required to fulfil the definition of Infection-related Ventilator-Associated Complications (IVAC).

statements.label: D
statements.text: A Ventilator-Associated Condition (VAC) requires a sustained increase in daily minimum PEEP of ≥3 cmH2O for ≥2 calendar days following ≥2 days of stable or decreasing PEEP.

questions.explanation: IVAC does not require a BAL culture — it requires VAC plus signs of possible infection (abnormal temperature or WBC) and new antibiotic therapy for ≥4 days, so that claim is false. For VAC via the FiO2 criterion, the threshold is ≥20 percentage points, not 15 — a common calculation trap. The PEEP-based VAC criteria and the stated purpose of the VAE framework are both correct.
questions.topic: VAE VAC IVAC PEEP ≥3 cmH2O FiO2 ≥20 points BAL not required

questions.question: Regarding the current classification of VAP prevention strategies as essential, additional, or not recommended:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Oral care with chlorhexidine remains a recommended essential practice due to consistent evidence of VAP rate reduction.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Subglottic secretion aspiration via specialised endotracheal tubes is classified as an additional approach, not an essential practice.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Daily tooth brushing without chlorhexidine is an essential VAP prevention practice.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Prone positioning is recommended as an additional VAP prevention strategy in mechanically ventilated patients.

questions.explanation: Chlorhexidine oral care has been reclassified as 'not recommended' because meta-analyses suggest it may increase mortality — the claim that it remains recommended is the most important clinical trap here. Prone positioning has also been re-classified as 'not recommended' specifically for VAP prevention, though it remains a cornerstone therapy for ARDS oxygenation — a critical distinction to hold in mind. Tooth brushing without chlorhexidine as an essential practice, and subglottic aspiration as an additional (not essential) approach, are both correct.
questions.topic: VAP prevention classification essential additional chlorhexidine not recommended prone positioning

questions.question: Regarding the prevention of catheter-related bloodstream infections (CRBSI) in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Chlorhexidine 2% is more effective than povidone-iodine as a skin antiseptic for CVC insertion.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Guidewire exchange is a safe and guideline-recommended approach when catheter-related infection is suspected.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The femoral site carries the lowest CRBSI risk among standard CVC insertion sites.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Inoculation of bacteria from the skin along the subcutaneous insertion tract is the most common route of CRBSI.

questions.explanation: Femoral cannulation is associated with higher CRBSI rates than subclavian and should be avoided, so the claim that it carries the lowest risk is false. Guidewire exchange is explicitly not recommended for CRBSI — the catheter must be removed and a new site selected, so calling it a safe, recommended approach is also false. Skin inoculation as the dominant route of infection, and chlorhexidine 2% superiority over povidone-iodine, are both correct.
questions.topic: CRBSI skin route CHX 2% femoral highest risk guidewire exchange not recommended

questions.question: Regarding antimicrobial catheters and parenteral nutrition in relation to CRBSI prevention:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The primary barrier to routine clinical adoption of antimicrobial catheters is their substantially higher cost compared with standard catheters.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Central venous access for parenteral nutrition does not increase CRBSI risk provided strict aseptic technique is maintained during administration.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Dedicating a single CVC lumen exclusively to total parenteral nutrition reduces CRBSI risk in patients requiring PN.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Minocycline-rifampicin impregnated catheters provide more durable infection protection (>3 weeks) than silver sulfadiazine-chlorhexidine impregnated catheters.

questions.explanation: The primary concern limiting routine adoption of antimicrobial catheters is the potential to drive multi-drug antimicrobial resistance, not cost — so cost as the main barrier is false. Parenteral nutrition through a CVC inherently increases CRBSI risk regardless of technique; a dedicated lumen and meticulous infection control reduce but do not eliminate that risk, so claiming aseptic technique alone prevents any increase is also false. Minocycline-rifampicin's superior durability and the benefit of dedicating a lumen to PN are both correct.
questions.topic: antimicrobial catheters minocycline rifampicin AMR concern PN dedicated lumen CRBSI risk

questions.question: Regarding the diagnosis and risk factors for catheter-associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI):

statements.label: A
statements.text: Routine surveillance urine cultures in all catheterised ICU patients are recommended for early CAUTI detection.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Female sex is a non-modifiable CAUTI risk factor due to the anatomically shorter urethra facilitating bacterial ascension.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Catheter dwell time is the most important modifiable risk factor for CAUTI.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Bacteriuria and pyuria together are sufficient to confirm a diagnosis of CAUTI in a catheterised patient.

questions.explanation: CAUTI is a clinical diagnosis; bacteriuria and pyuria together are common features of catheter colonisation and remain insufficient on their own to confirm infection, so that claim is false. Routine surveillance urine cultures are explicitly not recommended because of low specificity and the risk of triggering unnecessary antibiotic therapy, so that claim is also false. Catheter dwell time as the primary modifiable risk factor, and female sex as a non-modifiable one, are both correct.
questions.topic: CAUTI risk factors dwell time female sex diagnosis bacteriuria insufficient surveillance not recommended

questions.question: Regarding the prevention of catheter-associated urinary tract infections (CAUTI) in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The drainage bag should always be positioned below the level of the bladder to ensure unobstructed urine flow.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Nurse-driven catheter removal protocols are inferior to physician-directed catheter management in reducing CAUTI incidence.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Maintaining a closed urinary drainage system is an essential component of CAUTI prevention.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Urinary catheters should be routinely replaced at fixed intervals (e.g. every 7–10 days) to reduce CAUTI risk.

questions.explanation: Routine catheter replacement at fixed intervals is not recommended; catheters should be replaced only when sterility is compromised, so scheduled replacement is false. Nurse-driven protocols (NDPs) are evidenced to be superior, not inferior, to physician-directed management for reducing both catheter use and CAUTI incidence — a common misconception. Maintaining a closed drainage system and keeping the drainage bag below bladder level are both correct essential practices.
questions.topic: CAUTI prevention closed system bag below bladder routine replacement not recommended nurse-driven superior

questions.question: Regarding general HAI prevention strategies and organisational factors in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Standardised HAI surveillance (e.g. infections per 1,000 catheter-days) is a recommended component of ICU infection prevention programmes.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Prophylactic systemic antibiotics administered before endotracheal intubation or re-intubation are recommended to reduce VAP risk.

statements.label: C
statements.text: An unfavourable nurse-to-patient ratio is independently associated with a significantly increased risk of HAIs.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Ventilator circuits should be routinely replaced on a fixed weekly schedule to minimise VAP risk.

questions.explanation: Prophylactic antibiotics prior to intubation are not recommended for HAI prevention; their use promotes AMR without demonstrable benefit, so recommending them is false. Ventilator circuits should be changed only if visibly soiled or malfunctioning — not on a fixed weekly schedule, so that claim is also false. Standardised HAI surveillance and nurse-to-patient ratio as an independent HAI risk factor are both correct.
questions.topic: HAI surveillance nurse ratio prophylactic antibiotics not recommended ventilator circuit no routine change

questions.question: Regarding blood culture sampling practices when catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI) is suspected:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Staphylococcus aureus isolated from a single blood culture set is likely to represent contamination and should be interpreted cautiously before initiating treatment.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Routine prophylactic antibiotics for all ICU patients with CVCs in situ are recommended to prevent CRBSI.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Simultaneous collection of blood cultures from the inserted CVC and from a peripheral vein is recommended by the IDSA when CRBSI is suspected.

statements.label: D
statements.text: An inoculation volume of 8–10 mL of blood per culture bottle optimises diagnostic sensitivity.

questions.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus — along with Gram-negative Enterobacteriaceae and Candida — in even a single blood culture set should always be treated as true infection, not dismissed as contamination; only low-pathogenicity organisms such as coagulase-negative staphylococci in a single set raise contamination as a possibility, so dismissing S. aureus this way is a dangerous near-miss. Prophylactic antibiotics for CVC patients are not recommended and promote AMR, so that claim is also false. Simultaneous peripheral-plus-CVC cultures and 8–10 mL per bottle are both correct technique.
questions.topic: CRBSI blood cultures peripheral CVC Staph aureus not contamination prophylactic antibiotics not recommended

questions.question: An ICU multidisciplinary team is discussing whether earlier tracheostomy (within 7 days of intubation) should become standard practice for patients expected to need prolonged ventilatory support. Which statement about the evidence for early tracheostomy is most accurate?
options: A: Early tracheostomy reduces mortality primarily through earlier sedation cessation and improved secretion clearance
B: Early tracheostomy reduces VAP rates, mechanical ventilation duration, and ICU length of stay, but does not reduce mortality
C: Early tracheostomy provides no measurable benefit over tracheostomy performed after 14 days of intubation
D: Early tracheostomy benefits are limited to reduced sedative and analgesic requirements, with no measurable effect on VAP incidence or ventilator duration
E: Early tracheostomy increases VAP risk because the stoma itself acts as a new portal of microbial entry
questions.explanation: A meta-analysis of 17 randomised trials found early tracheostomy (within 7 days) associated with roughly a 40% reduction in VAP rates, shorter mechanical ventilation duration, and fewer ICU days — but no mortality difference. Assuming a mortality benefit follows automatically from these process improvements is the most common misconception; better tolerance and reduced sedation needs may contribute to those improvements but do not translate into a survival advantage. Dismissing timing as irrelevant, or narrowing the benefit to sedation alone, both understate the demonstrated VAP and ventilator-duration effect.
questions.topic: early tracheostomy 7 days VAP reduction no mortality benefit meta-analysis

questions.question: A hospital epidemiologist notes that peripheral venous catheters (PVCs) are rarely implicated in catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI) on a per-catheter basis compared with central venous catheters (CVCs), yet PVCs account for a disproportionately large share of the hospital's total CRBSI case count. What best explains this apparent discrepancy?
options: A: PVC-related bacteraemia predominantly reflects contamination during blood sampling rather than true catheter-related infection
B: Arterial catheters, not PVCs, are the true source of most bloodstream infections misattributed to peripheral lines
C: The sheer frequency and volume of PVC use means even a low per-catheter incidence produces a substantial absolute number of infections
D: PVCs carry an equivalent per-catheter CRBSI incidence to CVCs, so the total case count is simply proportional to insertion numbers
E: PVC-related bloodstream infections are systematically under-reported compared with CVC-related infections due to less rigorous surveillance definitions
questions.explanation: Despite a low incidence of PVC-related bloodstream infection on a per-catheter basis, the very high frequency of PVC use across a hospital means the resulting absolute number of infections is substantial — a volume effect rather than a per-device risk effect. Per-catheter incidence for PVCs remains genuinely lower than for CVCs, so treating the two as equivalent misreads the epidemiology in the opposite direction. Under-reporting and misattribution to sampling contamination or arterial lines are not the documented explanation for this pattern.
questions.topic: PVC low per-catheter incidence high absolute burden frequent use volume effect CRBSI epidemiology

questions.question: During ultrasound-guided internal jugular CVC insertion, a registrar suggests using a non-sterile probe cover 'just for a quick localisation scan' before switching to full sterile technique for the actual puncture. What is the most appropriate response?
options: A: A sterile probe and cable cover, plus sterile gel, must be used throughout ultrasound-guided insertion as part of maximal barrier precautions
B: A non-sterile cover is acceptable provided the probe never contacts the prepared skin field
C: Sterile probe technique is only required when inserting tunnelled, longer-term catheters
D: This is acceptable, as ultrasound localisation before needle insertion does not require sterile precautions
E: Ultrasound guidance should be avoided for CVC insertion in the ICU altogether because of the infection risk its probe and cable introduce into the sterile field
questions.explanation: When ultrasound is used during catheter insertion, full maximal barrier precautions must be maintained throughout, including a sterile cover for the probe and cable and sterile gel — not only during the puncture itself. Treating the localisation step as exempt from sterile technique, or contingent on skin contact, both risk contaminating the field before the line is even placed. Ultrasound itself is not a contraindication — it reduces complications such as misplacement and haematoma and may thereby reduce subsequent infection risk — and sterile probe technique applies regardless of whether the catheter is tunnelled.
questions.topic: ultrasound guided CVC insertion sterile probe cover maximal barrier precautions

questions.question: Regarding selective digestive/oropharyngeal decontamination (SDD/SOD) as a VAP prevention strategy:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Adding selective digestive decontamination (SDD) to SOD increases the observed mortality benefit to approximately 18% in low-AMR settings.

statements.label: B
statements.text: A cluster-randomised trial conducted in ICUs with higher antimicrobial resistance prevalence found a comparable mortality benefit from SDD/SOD to that seen in low-AMR settings.

statements.label: C
statements.text: The main clinical concern limiting broader use of SDD/SOD is the cost of the oral and parenteral antimicrobial agents involved.

statements.label: D
statements.text: In low-AMR settings (fewer than 5% ESBL-producing bloodstream isolates), selective oropharyngeal decontamination (SOD) alone has been associated with an approximate 16% reduction in hospital mortality.

questions.explanation: A meta-analysis of 6 cluster-randomised trials in low-AMR countries found SOD alone associated with an approximate 16% reduction in hospital mortality, rising to 18% when SDD was added — both true. A separate cluster-randomised trial performed in a higher-AMR setting showed no benefit, which is why the intervention is restricted to low-AMR ICUs — claiming a comparable benefit in high-AMR settings is false. The principal concern limiting adoption is the risk of driving antimicrobial resistance selection, not cost — echoing the same cost-versus-resistance trap seen with antimicrobial-impregnated catheters — so that claim is also false.
questions.topic: SDD SOD mortality benefit 16% 18% AMR setting restriction not cost concern
